title: Mitral Stenosis
section: Disorders of the Heart Muscles Valves and Pericardium

items.title: Rheumatic Fever as Primary Etiology of Mitral Stenosis
items.content: Rheumatic fever is the leading cause of mitral stenosis (MS), accounting for approximately 40% of cases in patients with rheumatic heart disease. The condition results from chronic inflammation that leads to fibrous tissue formation and calcification, causing leaflet thickening and commissural fusion.
keyPoints: Rheumatic fever is the leading cause of mitral stenosis (MS).
~40% of patients with rheumatic heart disease have pure or predominant MS due to rheumatic fever.
Chronic inflammation leads to fibrous tissue formation and calcification, causing leaflet thickening and commissural fusion.
items.reference: Mitral Stenosis · Mitral Stenosis | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOLOGY

items.title: Complications of Rheumatic Mitral Stenosis: Thrombus Formation and Embolization
items.content: In rheumatic mitral stenosis, calcification immobilizes the leaflets, narrowing the valve orifice further. This can lead to thrombus formation on the valve itself. However, in patients with atrial fibrillation (AF), thrombi more frequently arise from the dilated left atrium, especially within the LA appendage.
keyPoints: Calcification of the stenotic mitral valve immobilizes leaflets and narrows the orifice.
Thrombus formation can occur on the calcific valve itself.
In patients with atrial fibrillation (AF), thrombi more commonly arise from the dilated left atrium, particularly within the LA appendage.
items.reference: Mitral Stenosis · Mitral Stenosis | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOLOGY

items.title: Understanding the Hemodynamic Impact of Mitral Stenosis Severity
items.content: Mitral stenosis (MS) severity is characterized by a reduction in mitral valve orifice area, leading to elevated left atrioventricular pressure gradients. This elevation is crucial for maintaining normal cardiac output when severe MS (<1.5 cm²) occurs.
keyPoints: Normal mitral valve orifice area: 4–6 cm²
Significant obstruction defined as <2 cm²
Severe MS defined as <1.5 cm², requiring ~25 mmHg LA pressure for normal CO
Elevated pulmonary pressures reduce compliance and cause exertional dyspnea
Tachycardia increases transvalvular gradient by shortening diastole

items.title: The Role of Heart Rate in Mitral Stenosis Pathophysiology
items.content: In mitral stenosis, an increased heart rate exacerbates the transvalvular pressure gradient by reducing diastolic filling time. This effect is particularly pronounced during atrial fibrillation or other conditions causing tachycardia.
keyPoints: Heart rate affects flow across the mitral valve due to shortened diastole
Tachycardia increases transvalvular pressure gradient and LA pressure
LV diastolic pressure and EF remain normal in isolated MS
Atrial contraction pattern (a wave) is prominent with elevated LA pressures

items.title: Cardiac Output Changes in Mitral Stenosis (MS)
items.content: In severe MS, cardiac output (CO) is normal or nearly so at rest but increases subnormally during exertion. In very severe MS with a valve area <1 cm² and elevated pulmonary vascular resistance, CO is subnormal at rest and may not increase or even decline during activity.
keyPoints: Severe MS: Valve orifice 1–1.5 cm²; CO normal/resting but rises subnormally during exertion.
Very severe MS: Valve area <1 cm²; CO subnormal at rest, fails to rise or declines during activity.

items.title: Pulmonary Hypertension and Right Heart Failure in MS
items.content: Pulmonary hypertension in MS results from elevated left atrial pressure, pulmonary arteriolar constriction, interstitial edema, and obliterative changes. This leads to right ventricular enlargement, secondary tricuspid regurgitation, pulmonic regurgitation, and right-sided heart failure.
keyPoints: Pulmonary hypertension due to elevated LA pressure, arteriolar constriction, interstitial edema, and obliterative changes.
Consequences: RV enlargement, secondary TR, PR, right-sided heart failure.

items.title: Triggers and Progression of Symptoms in MS
items.content: Symptoms like dyspnea and cough can be triggered by sudden changes in heart rate or volume status. As MS progresses, lesser stressors cause symptoms, with persistent atrial fibrillation accelerating symptom progression. Hemoptysis is common due to pulmonary venous hypertension but rarely fatal.
keyPoints: Triggers: Sudden changes in heart rate/volume status (e.g., exertion, fever, AF).
Progression: Lesser stressors cause symptoms; persistent AF accelerates symptom progression.
Hemoptysis due to pulmonary venous hypertension is common but rarely fatal.

items.title: Auscultation Findings in Mitral Stenosis (MS)
items.content: In MS, auscultation reveals specific heart sounds that correlate with disease severity. The first heart sound (S₁) is accentuated early on, while the pulmonic component of the second heart sound (P₂) becomes more pronounced due to elevated pulmonary artery pressures (PAPs). A key feature is the opening snap (OS) of the mitral valve, which follows aortic valve closure (A₂) by 0.05–0.12 seconds; the interval between A₁ and OS inversely correlates with MS severity.
keyPoints: First heart sound (S₁) is accentuated early in MS.
Pulmonic component of second heart sound (P₂) is often accentuated due to elevated PAPs.
Opening snap (OS) follows aortic valve closure (A₂) by 0.05–0.12 seconds.
Interval between A₁ and OS inversely correlates with MS severity.
Diastolic murmur duration correlates with stenosis severity in patients with preserved cardiac output (CO).
Presystolic accentuation of the murmur occurs during atrial systole.

items.title: Associated Lesions and Murmurs in Severe Mitral Stenosis
items.content: Severe MS can lead to associated lesions such as functional tricuspid regurgitation (TR) and pulmonary regurgitation (PR), each producing distinct murmurs. A pansystolic murmur from TR is louder during inspiration (Carvallo's sign). The Graham Steell murmur, a high-pitched diastolic decrescendo blowing murmur along the left sternal border, results from PR due to severe pulmonary hypertension and may increase with inspiration.
keyPoints: Functional tricuspid regurgitation (TR) produces a pansystolic murmur louder during inspiration (Carvallo's sign).
Graham Steell murmur is a high-pitched diastolic decrescendo blowing murmur from pulmonary regurgitation (PR).
Occurs in severe MS with pulmonary hypertension.
May increase with inspiration and be accompanied by loud, palpable P₂.

items.title: ECG Findings in Mitral Stenosis (MS) and Associated Conditions
items.content: In MS with sinus rhythm, the ECG typically shows signs of left atrial enlargement. When severe pulmonary hypertension or tricuspid stenosis complicates MS, leading to right atrial enlargement, the P wave becomes tall and peaked in lead II and upright in lead V. The QRS complex is usually normal unless there's severe pulmonary hypertension, which may cause right axis deviation and right ventricular hypertrophy.
keyPoints: P wave suggests left atrial enlargement in MS with sinus rhythm.
Tall and peaked P wave in lead II and upright in lead V indicates severe pulmonary hypertension or tricuspid stenosis complicating MS.
QRS complex is usually normal; right axis deviation and RV hypertrophy may occur with severe pulmonary hypertension.

items.title: Chest X-Ray Changes in Mitral Stenosis
items.content: Early changes on a chest X-ray for MS include straightening of the upper left border of the cardiac silhouette, prominence of the main pulmonary arteries, dilation of the upper lobe pulmonary veins, and posterior displacement of the esophagus due to an enlarged left atrium. Kerley B lines, which are fine, dense, horizontal lines in the lower and mid-lung fields, indicate interlobular septal and lymphatic distention with edema when resting mean left atrial pressure exceeds approximately 20 mmHg.
keyPoints: Early chest X-ray changes: straightening of upper left cardiac border, prominence of main pulmonary arteries, dilation of upper lobe pulmonary veins, posterior displacement of esophagus.
Kerley B lines indicate interlobular septal and lymphatic distention with edema when resting mean LA pressure exceeds ~20 mmHg.

items.title: Differentiating Mitral Stenosis from Other Conditions
items.content: Mitral stenosis (MS) can be confused with other cardiac conditions due to overlapping clinical features. Key differentiators include the timing and characteristics of murmurs, presence or absence of certain heart sounds, and response to pharmacologic agents.
keyPoints: In isolated mitral regurgitation (MR), diastolic murmur commences slightly later than in MS; LV enlargement is often evident.
An apical pansystolic murmur of at least grade III/VI intensity and an S3 suggest significant MR, not MS.
Austin Flint murmur associated with severe aortic regurgitation (AR) does not intensify in pre-systole and softens with arterial vasodilators like amyl nitrite.
Tricuspid stenosis (TS), when present, increases the diastolic murmur with inspiration and delays the y descent in the jugular venous pulse.

items.title: Identifying Atrial Septal Defect vs. Mitral Stenosis
items.content: Atrial septal defect (ASD) may mimic MS, but certain clinical and diagnostic features help distinguish between the two.
keyPoints: Both ASD and MS can show RV enlargement and accentuated pulmonary vascularity on ECG and chest x-ray.
Absence of left atrial enlargement, Kerley B lines, and fixed splitting of S2 with a mid-systolic murmur at the upper left sternal border favor ASD over MS.
Large left-to-right shunts in ASD may cause functional tricuspid stenosis due to enhanced diastolic flow.
An incomplete right bundle branch block pattern on ECG is often present in ASD.

items.title: Role of Cardiac Catheterization in Discrepancies and Associated Lesions
items.content: Cardiac catheterization is useful when there's a discrepancy between clinical findings and noninvasive tests like TEE or exercise echocardiography, as well as for assessing associated lesions such as AS and AR. It aids in evaluating patients with recurring or worsening symptoms post-mitral valve intervention.
keyPoints: Used to resolve discrepancies between clinical and noninvasive findings
Assesses associated lesions like aortic stenosis (AS) and regurgitation (AR)
Helpful for patients with recurrent/worsening symptoms after mitral valve interventions
items.reference: Mitral Stenosis · CARDIAC CATHETERIZATION | Mitral Stenosis (Fig. 274-2)

items.title: Anticoagulation and Cardioversion in AF Patients with MS
items.content: In patients with rheumatic mitral stenosis (MS) and atrial fibrillation (AF), vitamin K antagonists are recommended to reduce stroke risk, targeting an INR of 2–3. For recent onset AF without severe MS needing immediate intervention, reversion to sinus rhythm is indicated after anticoagulation for at least 3 weeks or urgent heparinization with TEE to rule out LA thrombus.
keyPoints: Vitamin K antagonists target INR of 2–3 for patients with AF and MS
Indicated indefinitely in MS with AF, history of thromboembolism, or LA thrombus
Cardioversion after 3 weeks of anticoagulation or urgent heparinization with TEE if needed
Conversion to sinus rhythm is challenging in severe MS with large LA or long-standing AF
items.reference: Mitral Stenosis · CARDIAC CATHETERIZATION | Mitral Stenosis (Fig. 274-2)

items.title: Indications for Mitral Commissurotomy in Severe MS
items.content: Mitral commissurotomy is indicated for symptomatic patients with isolated severe mitral stenosis (MS) who have a valve area <~1 cm²/m² or <1.5 cm², unless contraindicated.
keyPoints: Symptomatic patients in NYHA class II–IV
Valve area <~1 cm²/m² body surface area or <1.5 cm² for normal-sized adults
Percutaneous or surgical commissurotomy options

items.title: Criteria and Outcomes of Percutaneous Mitral Balloon Commissurotomy (PMBC)
items.content: PMBC is preferred for younger patients with pliable valves, showing high event-free survival rates. It requires specific patient criteria such as minimal leaflet calcification and absence of LA thrombus.
keyPoints: Percutaneous approach with catheter-directed balloon
Ideal candidates: pliable leaflets, <2+/4+ MR severity
Event-free survival 80–90% over 3–7 years in patients <45 years

items.title: Surgical Commissurotomy and Mitral Valve Replacement Indications
items.content: Surgical commissurotomy is necessary when PMBC is not feasible or unsuccessful, especially in cases of restenosis. MVR is indicated for severe MS with significant MR or distorted valves.
keyPoints: Surgical approach needed if PMBC fails or for restenosis
MVR required for significant associated MR or distorted valves
Perioperative mortality ~2% for surgical commissurotomy, 2–5% for MVR

items.question: Which condition is the leading cause of mitral stenosis?
items.explanation: Rheumatic fever is the leading cause of mitral stenosis.
items.reference: Mitral Stenosis · Mitral Stenosis | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOLOGY

items.question: What percentage of patients with rheumatic heart disease have pure or predominant mitral stenosis?
items.explanation: ~40% of all patients with rheumatic heart disease and a history of rheumatic fever have pure or predominant MS.
items.reference: Mitral Stenosis · Mitral Stenosis | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOLOGY

items.question: In which regions does mitral stenosis remain a major problem despite its decline in temperate climates?
options: Sub-Saharan Africa, India, Southeast Asia, and Oceania
items.explanation: Mitral stenosis remains a major problem in sub-Saharan Africa, India, Southeast Asia, and Oceania.
items.reference: Mitral Stenosis · Mitral Stenosis | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOLOGY

items.question: What mitral valve orifice area is considered 'severe' mitral stenosis?
items.explanation: Severe MS is defined when the mitral valve opening is reduced to <1.5 cm².

items.question: What LA pressure is required to maintain normal cardiac output in severe mitral stenosis?
items.explanation: An LA pressure of ~25 mmHg is required to maintain normal cardiac output in severe MS.

items.question: What hemodynamic hallmark characterizes mitral stenosis?
options: Abnormally elevated left atrioventricular pressure gradient
items.explanation: The hemodynamic hallmark of MS is an abnormally elevated left atrioventricular pressure gradient.

items.question: In patients with severe mitral stenosis (MS), how does cardiac output (CO) behave during exertion?
options: It remains subnormal and may decline.
It is normal at rest but rises subnormally.
items.explanation: In severe MS, CO is normal or almost so at rest but rises subnormally during exertion.

items.question: Which of the following can precipitate dyspnea and cough in patients with mild elevations of left atrial pressure due to mitral stenosis?
items.explanation: Dyspnea and cough can be precipitated by sudden changes like severe exertion, excitement, fever, etc., in patients with mild elevations of LA pressure.

items.question: What is a common consequence of persistent atrial fibrillation (AF) in mitral stenosis?
items.explanation: Persistent AF often marks a turning point and is associated with an acceleration in the rate at which symptoms progress.

items.question: What condition can result from rupture of pulmonary-bronchial venous connections due to elevated left atrial pressures?
items.explanation: Rupture of pulmonary-bronchial venous connections secondary to elevated LA pressures can result in hemoptysis.

items.question: Which of the following findings is most likely to be observed in the jugular venous pulse of a patient with severe mitral stenosis (MS) and sinus rhythm?
options: Prominent c waves due to vigorous right ventricular systole.
Absent a waves indicating atrial fibrillation.
Prominent a waves due to vigorous right atrial systole.
Absence of v waves suggesting tricuspid regurgitation.
items.explanation: Severe MS with sinus rhythm often results in prominent a waves in the jugular venous pulse due to vigorous right atrial contraction against increased resistance.

items.question: What is the typical time interval between the sound of aortic valve closure (A2) and the opening snap (OS) in mitral stenosis?
items.explanation: The opening snap of the mitral valve typically follows aortic valve closure by 0.05–0.12 seconds, inversely related to the severity of MS.

items.question: In patients with severe pulmonary hypertension associated with mitral stenosis, which murmur is characterized by increasing intensity during inspiration?
options: A pansystolic murmur of aortic regurgitation.
The Graham Steell murmur of pulmonary regurgitation.
A diastolic rumbling murmur at the apex.
An ejection systolic murmur along the right sternal border.
items.explanation: In severe pulmonary hypertension, a pansystolic murmur due to functional tricuspid regurgitation increases in intensity during inspiration (Carvallo's sign).

items.question: Which ECG finding is typically associated with severe pulmonary hypertension complicating mitral stenosis (MS)?
options: Right axis deviation and right ventricular hypertrophy
Left axis deviation and left atrial enlargement
Inverted T waves in leads V1-V4
items.explanation: Severe pulmonary hypertension with MS often presents with right axis deviation and RV hypertrophy on ECG.

items.question: What does transthoracic echocardiography (TTE) provide in the assessment of mitral stenosis?
options: Only measurements of mitral inflow velocity
Assessment of left ventricular and right ventricular function, chamber sizes, and PA systolic pressure estimation
Exclusively anatomic suitability for percutaneous mitral balloon commissurotomy
items.explanation: TTE provides a comprehensive assessment including LV and RV function, chamber sizes, and PA systolic pressure estimation.

items.question: When is transesophageal echocardiography (TEE) especially indicated in the management of mitral stenosis?
options: When transthoracic echocardiography is inadequate for guiding management decisions
For measuring mitral inflow velocity during diastolic filling
To evaluate pulmonary artery systolic pressure
items.explanation: TEE is especially indicated when TTE is inadequate for guiding management decisions, such as excluding LA thrombus before PMBC.

items.question: Which feature helps differentiate an Austin Flint murmur from mitral stenosis?
options: It becomes softer with administration of amyl nitrite or other arterial vasodilators.
There is evidence of left ventricular enlargement.
The diastolic murmur increases with inspiration.
items.explanation: An Austin Flint murmur becomes softer with the use of amyl nitrite, unlike a mitral stenosis murmur.

items.question: What finding is more suggestive of an atrial septal defect rather than mitral stenosis?
options: Fixed splitting of S2 with a mid-systolic murmur at the upper left sternal border.
Diastolic murmur that increases with inspiration.
items.explanation: Atrial septal defects are characterized by fixed splitting of S2 and a mid-systolic murmur at the upper left sternal border.

items.question: Which condition may present with systemic symptoms like weight loss, fever, anemia, and elevated serum IgG and IL-6 concentrations?
items.explanation: Left atrial myxomas can present with systemic symptoms such as weight loss, fever, anemia, and elevated serum IgG and IL-6 concentrations.

items.question: Which medication is recommended indefinitely for patients with mitral stenosis who have atrial fibrillation, a history of thromboembolism, or demonstrated left atrial thrombus?
items.explanation: Vitamin K antagonists are recommended indefinitely for these patients to reduce the risk of stroke or systemic embolism.
items.reference: Mitral Stenosis · CARDIAC CATHETERIZATION | Mitral Stenosis (Fig. 274-2)

items.question: What is a controversial practice in patients with mitral stenosis and sinus rhythm?
items.explanation: The routine use of a vitamin K antagonist in patients with sinus rhythm and LA enlargement is controversial.
items.reference: Mitral Stenosis · CARDIAC CATHETERIZATION | Mitral Stenosis (Fig. 274-2)

items.question: What should be done before cardioversion if it is indicated more urgently in a patient with mitral stenosis?
options: Provide intravenous heparin and perform TEE
items.explanation: Intravenous heparin should be provided and TEE performed to exclude the presence of LA thrombus before urgent cardioversion.
items.reference: Mitral Stenosis · CARDIAC CATHETERIZATION | Mitral Stenosis (Fig. 274-2)

items.question: Which patients are ideal candidates for percutaneous mitral commissurotomy (PMBC)?
options: Patients with severe MS and significant subvalvular scarring.
Patients with pliable leaflets, little or no commissural calcium, and no LA thrombus.
Patients with NYHA class I symptoms and a mitral valve area >2 cm².
Patients with severe MR of ≥3+/4+ severity.
items.explanation: Ideal candidates for PMBC have pliable leaflets, little or no commissural calcium, and no LA thrombus.

items.question: What is the primary imaging modality used to exclude LA thrombus before performing PMBC?
items.explanation: TEE is performed routinely to exclude LA thrombus and assess MR at the time of the scheduled procedure.

items.question: What defines a successful commissurotomy?
options: A 25% reduction in the mean mitral valve gradient.
An increase in the mitral valve area by 50%.
A 50% reduction in the mean mitral valve gradient and doubling of the mitral valve area.
Complete elimination of any associated MR.
items.explanation: Successful commissurotomy is defined by a 50% reduction in the mean mitral valve gradient and a doubling of the mitral valve area.

items.question: In which patients is mitral valve replacement (MVR) necessary?
options: Patients with mild MS and no associated MR.
Patients with severe MS, significant associated MR, or severely distorted valves.
Asymptomatic patients with a mitral valve area >1.5 cm².
items.explanation: MVR is necessary in patients with MS and significant associated MR, or those with severely distorted valves.

items.statement: In rheumatic mitral stenosis, the initial insult to the valve is due to calcification.
items.explanation: The initial insult in rheumatic MS is due to rheumatic fever, not calcification.
items.reference: Mitral Stenosis · Mitral Stenosis | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOLOGY

items.statement: Thrombus formation and arterial embolization can arise from the dilated left atrium in patients with atrial fibrillation.
items.explanation: In AF, thrombi more frequently arise from the dilated LA, particularly from within the LA appendage.
items.reference: Mitral Stenosis · Mitral Stenosis | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOLOGY

items.statement: In isolated mitral stenosis, the LV ejection fraction (EF) is abnormal.
items.explanation: The LV diastolic pressure and ejection fraction are normal in isolated MS.

items.statement: Tachycardia can increase the transvalvular pressure gradient in mitral stenosis.
items.explanation: An increase in heart rate shortens diastole, diminishing time for flow across the mitral valve and augmenting the transvalvular pressure gradient.

items.statement: Pulmonary hypertension in mitral stenosis is solely due to passive backward transmission of elevated left atrial pressure.
items.explanation: Pulmonary hypertension results from multiple factors including LA pressure, arteriolar constriction, interstitial edema, and obliterative changes.

items.statement: Systemic embolization is more common in patients with atrial fibrillation and reduced cardiac output.
items.explanation: Systemic embolization occurs more frequently in patients with AF, those over 65 years of age, and those with a reduced CO.

items.statement: The duration of the diastolic murmur correlates with the severity of mitral stenosis in patients with preserved cardiac output.
items.explanation: In MS, a longer diastolic murmur duration indicates more severe stenosis when cardiac output is maintained.

items.statement: The Graham Steell murmur of pulmonary regurgitation can be distinguished from the murmur of aortic regurgitation by its decrease in intensity during inspiration.
items.explanation: The Graham Steell murmur increases in intensity with inspiration, unlike typical murmurs of aortic regurgitation.

items.statement: Kerley B lines on a chest X-ray are indicative of distention of interlobular septae and lymphatics with edema when the resting mean LA pressure exceeds approximately 20 mmHg.
items.explanation: Kerley B lines result from increased LA pressure leading to edema, visible on a chest X-ray.

items.statement: The QRS complex is usually abnormal in patients with mitral stenosis and severe pulmonary hypertension.
items.explanation: The QRS complex is typically normal, but abnormalities can occur with severe pulmonary hypertension.

items.statement: An apical pansystolic murmur of at least grade III/VI intensity suggests significant mitral regurgitation.
items.explanation: Significant MR is suggested by an apical pansystolic murmur of at least grade III/VI intensity.

items.statement: In tricuspid stenosis, the diastolic murmur decreases with inspiration.
items.explanation: The diastolic murmur of tricuspid stenosis increases with inspiration.

items.statement: In patients with rheumatic mitral stenosis and atrial fibrillation, rivaroxaban is recommended over vitamin K antagonist therapy.
items.explanation: A randomized trial showed a higher incidence of death among patients treated with rivaroxaban compared to those on vitamin K antagonists.
items.reference: Mitral Stenosis · CARDIAC CATHETERIZATION | Mitral Stenosis (Fig. 274-2)

items.statement: Cardioversion should be undertaken after at least 3 weeks of anticoagulant treatment to a therapeutic INR in patients with mitral stenosis.
items.explanation: This is recommended unless cardioversion is more urgent, in which case intravenous heparin and TEE are used.
items.reference: Mitral Stenosis · CARDIAC CATHETERIZATION | Mitral Stenosis (Fig. 274-2)

items.statement: Percutaneous mitral commissurotomy (PMBC) is recommended for asymptomatic patients with mild or moderate stenosis.
items.explanation: Commissurotomy is not recommended for asymptomatic patients and/or those with mild or moderate stenosis.

items.statement: The perioperative mortality rate for surgical mitral valve repair procedures is approximately 2%.
items.explanation: The perioperative mortality rate for open surgical commissurotomy using cardiopulmonary bypass is ~2%.

items.assertion: Orthopnea develops as mitral stenosis progresses.
items.reason: Lesser degrees of stress precipitate dyspnea, limiting daily activities and leading to orthopnea.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; lesser stress leading to dyspnea explains the development of orthopnea.

items.assertion: Pulmonary compliance increases during exercise in mitral stenosis.
items.reason: Pulmonary capillary pressure rises, causing further reduction in pulmonary compliance.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; pulmonary compliance falls as pulmonary capillary pressure rises during exercise.

items.assertion: Transthoracic echocardiography (TTE) provides an estimation of the PA systolic pressure based on tricuspid regurgitant jet velocity.
items.reason: This is achieved through spectral Doppler imaging which measures flow velocities across heart valves.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains how TTE estimates PA systolic pressure.

items.assertion: Atrial septal defects can result in functional tricuspid stenosis.
items.reason: Large left-to-right shunts in atrial septal defects enhance diastolic flow, leading to functional tricuspid stenosis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Percutaneous mitral commissurotomy (PMBC) should be performed by a skilled operator in a high-volume center.
items.reason: The procedure has excellent outcomes with low morbidity and mortality when done correctly.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion.

items.assertion: Mitral valve replacement (MVR) should be avoided in patients with severe MS.
items.reason: MVR has long-term complications, and less invasive options like commissurotomy are preferred if possible.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; MVR is necessary for certain cases of severe MS. The reason is true.
